.\outputs\audits\I-redact-001\redaction_fixture.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. Actual distribution: T1=2%, T3=1%, T4=5%, T5=0%, T6=4%, T7=5%, UNKNOWN=82%.\nCorpus adequacy: decision=proceed, 8/8 thresholds met.\nCompleteness checklist: 6/6 topics covered.\n\n## Capability disclosures\nQuantified trade-off analysis was ENABLED but did not contribute to this report (no verified quantified output); 249 sourced numbers were extracted but not modeled into a verified quantified comparison.\n\n## Contradiction disclosures\nThe contradiction detector flagged 2 numeric disagreements across the evidence pool. Most are extraction artifacts produced by grouping different measured endpoints, units, sub-populations, time windows, or comparators under the same subject/predicate label. The detector does NOT adjudicate by endpoint, population, timepoint, or source tier; raw detector output is available in `contradictions.json`. We must preserve the [#ev:ev_004:5700-6500] marker exactly. We cannot introduce new specifics not in the span. The span does mention: \"a 0.063% increase in the high-skilled labour force\", \"a 0.001% increase in the medium-skilled labour force\", \"a 0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force\", \"employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001%\", and \"AI increases the demand for high- and medium-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour.\" It does not mention China, provincial panel data, 2010-2019, N=310. So we must drop those specifics. Also, the original sentence says \"0.001% decrease in low-skilled employment\" but the span says \"0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force\" and \"employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001%\". So we can say a decrease in low-skilled employment share or something. The span also says \"a 0.001% increase in the medium-skilled labour force\" which the original omitted. The original only mentioned high-skilled increase and low-skilled decrease. But we can include medium-skilled if we want, but we must not add new specifics that aren't in the span. The span does say \"AI increases the demand for high- and medium-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour.\" So we can make a claim about that. The original said \"indicating a skill-biased shift in labor demand\" which is supported by the span's statement \"This indicates that AI increases the demand for high- and medium-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour.\" So we can keep that.\n\nWe need to rewrite the sentence to only include claims that are directly supported by the span. So we can say something like: \"A one-unit increase in AI was associated with a 0.063% increase in high-skilled employment and a 0.001% decrease in low-skilled employment, indicating a skill-biased shift in labor demand [#ev:ev_004:5700-6500].\" But wait, the span says \"0.001% increase in the medium-skilled labour force\" and \"0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force\" and \"employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001%\". The original said \"0.001% decrease in low-skilled employment\" which is not exactly the same as the span. The span says a 0.001-unit decrease in growth, and a 0.001% decrease in employment share. So we could say \"a 0.001% decrease in the employment share of low-skilled workers\" or \"a decrease in low-skilled employment\". But the span does not explicitly say \"0.001% decrease in low-skilled employment\" as a level; it says a decrease in growth and a decrease in share. To be safe, we can use the qualitative claim: \"AI increases the demand for high-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour\" and drop the exact numbers if they aren't perfectly matched. But the span does give numbers: 0.063% increase in high-skilled labour force, and 0.001% decrease in employment share of lower-skilled workers. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[2]\n\n### Prebiotic / dietary-fiber intake and colorectal-cancer risk\n\nExposure: dietary fibre and whole grains.[1] Population: more than 1.7 million participants.[1] Outcome: colorectal cancer.[1] Dose response: 0.90 (0.86 to 0.94) for each 10 g/day intake.[1] The pooled analysis included more than 1.7 million participants.[1] In the dose-response analysis, each 10 g/day increment in intake was associated with a relative risk of 0.90.[1] The 95% confidence interval for this estimate ranged from 0.86 to 0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. Three high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree sharply on the effect on ttr (relative difference 12275.0%), on ttr at 0.5 mg (25.0% difference), and on weight loss (212.5% difference), indicating substantial inconsistency in key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, potentially omitting earlier foundational research and limiting the temporal scope of the analysis.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 feb9fd7172651bfc...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 21}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 375 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 365 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]\n- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]\n- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. .\tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template.   524: def _recency_epsilon() -> float:
  525:     """Relevance band width `PG_SELECT_RECENCY_EPSILON` (default 0.05).
  526:     epsilon <= 0 → exact-score-tie mode (band = raw score)."""
  527:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SELECT_RECENCY_EPSILON", "").strip()
  528:     if not raw:
  529:         return _RECENCY_EPSILON_DEFAULT
  530:     try:
  531:         return float(raw)
  532:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  533:         return _RECENCY_EPSILON_DEFAULT
  534: 
  535: 
  536: def _row_year(row: dict[str, Any]) -> int | None:
  537:     """Publication year from `row['year']` or `row['metadata']['year']`.
  538:     Returns None if absent / non-numeric / outside [1900, 2100]. No network."""
  539:     val = row.get("year")
  540:     if val is None:
  541:         meta = row.get("metadata")
  542:         if isinstance(meta, dict):
  543:             val = meta.get("year")
  544:     if val is None:
  545:         return None
  546:     try:
  547:         year = int(val)
  548:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  549:         return None
  550:     if year < _RECENCY_MIN_YEAR or year > _RECENCY_MAX_YEAR:
  551:         return None
  552:     return year
  553: 
  554: 
  555: def _relevance_band(score: float, epsilon: float) -> float:
  556:     """Bucket a relevance score into a band. Higher score → higher band.
  557:     epsilon <= 0 → exact score (only exact ties share a band)."""
  558:     if epsilon <= 0:
  559:         return score
  560:     return math.floor(score / epsilon)
  561: 
  562: 
  563: def _year_sort_value(row: dict[str, Any]) -> int:
  564:     """Ascending-sort value for 'newer first': negated year; a missing year
  565:     maps below any real year so undated rows sort LAST within a band (but are
  566:     never excluded)."""
  567:     year = _row_year(row)
  568:     return -(year if year is not None else _RECENCY_MIN_YEAR - 1)
  569: 
  570: 
  571: def _relevance_recency_key(
  572:     item: tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]],
  573:     enabled: bool,
  574:     epsilon: float,
  575: ) -> tuple:
  576:     """Sort fragment ranking by relevance then (soft) recency, or by raw
  577:     relevance when disabled. Callers append the original index for full
  578:     determinism. Disabled → `(-score,)`, byte-identical to the prior key."""
  579:     _idx, score, _tier, row = item
  580:     if not enabled:
  581:         return (-score,)
  582:     # Within a relevance band, newer year first; then EXACT score as the
  583:     # sub-tiebreaker. Because the band is monotonic in score and exact score
  584:     # breaks within-band ties, an all-undated corpus reproduces the prior
  585:     # pure-(-score) order EXACTLY — recency only reorders DATED same-band rows.
  586:     return (-_relevance_band(score, epsilon), _year_sort_value(row), -score)
  587: 
  588: 
  589: def _recency_telemetry_note(
  590:     scored: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
  591:     epsilon: float,
  592: ) -> str:
  593:     """Note emitted ONLY when the recency tiebreaker is enabled."""
  594:     dated = sum(1 for s in scored if _row_year(s[3]) is not None)
  595:     return (
  596:         f"recency_tiebreak enabled epsilon={epsilon} "
  597:         f"dated={dated}/{len(scored)}"
  598:     )

  601: # ── #956 (S2, 2026-05-30): source-diversity passes ──────────────────────────
  602: # Tier quota != topical diversity: 20 T1 RCTs all on ONE sub-topic satisfy the
  603: # T1 quota while starving the other sub-topics, tanking per-sub-topic coverage.
  604: # Two SOFT post-selection passes operate ONLY on post-floor slack and ALWAYS
  605: # preserve tier minimums (Codex brief-gate P2). Because the selected set sits
  606: # exactly at the tier quotas, every quota-preserving swap is SAME-TIER: a
  607: # diversity swap replaces a same-tier NON-reserved relevance-fill row with a
  608: # same-tier pool row, so no tier ever drops below quota and no floor-reserved
  609: # (M-42e/M-51/M-42c/M-42d) or sub-query-reserved row is ever evicted. Both
  610: # passes run on the truncating main path only (short-pool keeps every row, so
  611: # diversity is already maximal there). Codex rulings: k=1, cap_frac=0.5,
  612: # reservation BEFORE domain cap.
  613: _SUBQUERY_K_DEFAULT = 1
  614: _DOMAIN_CAP_FRAC_DEFAULT = 0.5
  615: 
  616: 
  617: def _env_flag_on(name: str) -> bool:
  618:     return os.environ.get(name, "1").strip().lower() not in ("0", "false", "no", "off")
  619: 
  620: 
  621: def _subquery_reserve_config() -> tuple[bool, int]:
  622:     enabled = _env_flag_on("PG_SELECT_SUBQUERY_RESERVE")
  623:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SELECT_SUBQUERY_K", "").strip()
  624:     try:
  625:         k = int(raw) if raw else _SUBQUERY_K_DEFAULT
  626:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  627:         k = _SUBQUERY_K_DEFAULT
  628:     return enabled, max(1, k)
  629: 
  630: 
  631: def _domain_cap_config() -> tuple[bool, float]:
  632:     enabled = _env_flag_on("PG_SELECT_DOMAIN_CAP")
  633:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SELECT_DOMAIN_CAP_FRAC", "").strip()
  634:     try:
  635:         frac = float(raw) if raw else _DOMAIN_CAP_FRAC_DEFAULT
  636:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  637:         frac = _DOMAIN_CAP_FRAC_DEFAULT
  638:     return enabled, frac
  639: 
  640: 
  641: # ── I-perm-003 (#1197): corpus-size-scaled evidence-selection budget ─────────
  642: # The generator-facing cap is a FIXED `max_rows` (PG_LIVE_MAX_EV_TO_GEN, default
  643: # 20 — #1070/#1078). At 1000-URL retrieval the fixed cap truncates the pool to a
  644: # constant slice regardless of how much best-ranked evidence the corpus actually
  645: # holds. This PREVENTATIVE knob scales the selection budget WITH the pool size so
  646: # a larger corpus feeds proportionally more BEST-ranked rows (the existing
  647: # tier-balanced + relevance/recency truncation already picks best-ranked, never
  648: # first-N — this only raises the budget it operates under).
  649: #
  650: # HONEST SCOPE: on the beatboth8 corpus the selector drops 0 sources (the ~90%
  651: # loss is UPSTREAM extraction, owned by I-perm-007), so this changes nothing
  652: # there. It is a forward guard for when the upstream pool is genuinely large.
  653: #
  654: # DEFAULT OFF. When `PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE` is unset/0/false/no/off the helper
  655: # returns `base_max_rows` UNCHANGED and `select_evidence_for_generation` is
  656: # byte-identical to the prior behaviour (no reassignment, no telemetry).
  657: #
  658: # FLOOR semantics: effective = max(base_max_rows, ceil(pool_size * frac)),
  659: # optionally clamped to a ceiling. The `max(...)` guarantees scaling NEVER drops
  660: # the budget below the operator/code cap (a small pool with a low frac keeps the
  661: # existing cap — never a regression).
  662: _SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC_DEFAULT = 0.30
  663: # 0 = no ceiling (scale unbounded with the pool). A positive value clamps the
  664: # scaled budget so an enormous pool can't blow past an operator-set ceiling.
  665: _SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING_DEFAULT = 0
  666: 
  667: 
  668: def _selection_scale_enabled() -> bool:
  669:     """Flag `PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE` (default OFF). ON only on explicit
  670:     truthy ('1'/'true'/'yes'/'on'). OFF → byte-identical prior selection AND
  671:     no scaling telemetry. Inverse default of `_env_flag_on` ON-by-default."""
  672:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE", "0").strip().lower()
  673:     return raw in ("1", "true", "yes", "on")
  674: 
  675: 
  676: def _selection_scale_frac() -> float:
  677:     """Budget-per-pool-row fraction `PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC`
  678:     (default 0.30). Non-positive / unparseable → default (FAIL SOFT to a sane
  679:     positive fraction; a 0 frac would make scaling a no-op via the floor)."""
  680:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC", "").strip()
  681:     if not raw:
  682:         return _SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC_DEFAULT
  683:     try:
  684:         frac = float(raw)
  685:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  686:         return _SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC_DEFAULT
  687:     # Reject non-finite (inf/nan): a positive `inf` parses but would OverflowError in
  688:     # `math.ceil(pool_size * frac)` (Codex iter-1 P2). FAIL SOFT to the sane default.
  689:     return frac if (frac > 0 and math.isfinite(frac)) else _SELECTION_SCALE_FRAC_DEFAULT
  690: 
  691: 
  692: def _selection_scale_ceiling() -> int:
  693:     """Optional absolute ceiling `PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING`
  694:     (default 0 = no ceiling). Clamps the scaled budget so an enormous pool can't
  695:     overshoot an operator cap. Values <= 0 / unparseable → no ceiling."""
  696:     raw = os.environ.get("PG_SWEEP_SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING", "").strip()
  697:     if not raw:
  698:         return _SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING_DEFAULT
  699:     try:
  700:         ceiling = int(raw)
  701:     except (ValueError, TypeError):
  702:         return _SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING_DEFAULT
  703:     return ceiling if ceiling > 0 else _SELECTION_SCALE_CEILING_DEFAULT
  704: 
  705: 
  706: def _scaled_max_rows(pool_size: int, base_max_rows: int) -> tuple[int, str | None]:
  707:     """Corpus-size-scaled selection budget (I-perm-003, default OFF).
  708: 
  709:     Returns ``(effective_max_rows, note)``. When the flag is OFF, returns
  710:     ``(base_max_rows, None)`` — the caller MUST treat that as a byte-identical
  711:     no-op (no telemetry note appended). When ON, returns the FLOOR-guarded scaled
  712:     budget ``max(base_max_rows, ceil(pool_size * frac))`` (optionally clamped to a
  713:     ceiling) plus a single telemetry string for the selection notes.
  714:     """
  715:     if not _selection_scale_enabled():
  716:         return base_max_rows, None
  717:     frac = _selection_scale_frac()
  718:     ceiling = _selection_scale_ceiling()
  719:     scaled = math.ceil(pool_size * frac)
  720:     effective = max(base_max_rows, scaled)
  721:     clamped = False
  722:     if ceiling > 0 and effective > ceiling:
  723:         # Never below the base cap even when the ceiling is < base (floor wins).
  724:         effective = max(base_max_rows, ceiling)
  725:         clamped = True
  726:     note = (
  727:         f"selection_scale pool={pool_size} frac={frac} "
  728:         f"base_max_rows={base_max_rows} scaled={scaled} "
  729:         f"effective={effective} ceiling={ceiling or 'none'}"
  730:         f"{' clamped' if clamped else ''}"
  731:     )
  732:     return effective, note
  733:

  736:     """The sub-query that surfaced the row (`query_origin`), or `_unlabeled`."""
  737:     return str(row.get("query_origin") or "") or "_unlabeled"

 1076: def _relevance_floor_selection(
 1077:     *,
 1078:     scored: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
 1079:     relevance_floor: float,
 1080:     full_counts: dict[str, int],
 1081:     primary_trial_anchors: list[str] | None,
 1082: ) -> EvidenceSelection:
 1083:     """I-meta-005 Phase 5 (#989): relevance-floor selection (no max_rows cap).
 1084: 
 1085:     Keep EVERY row whose lexical relevance >= ``relevance_floor``, PLUS any row
 1086:     matching a primary trial anchor (floor-EXEMPT — a relevant primary RCT must
 1087:     never be dropped on a low lexical score). Ranked by ``relevance x authority``
 1088:     (authority = the row's ``authority_score`` sidecar, default 1.0). Each kept
 1089:     row is stamped with the additive ``selection_relevance`` float so
 1090:     ``finding_dedup`` picks representatives on the IDENTICAL score (no recompute
 1091:     drift). Pure; returns SHALLOW COPIES (never mutates the caller's rows).
 1092:     """
 1093:     anchors = list(primary_trial_anchors or [])
 1094: 
 1095:     def _is_anchor(row: dict[str, Any]) -> bool:
 1096:         return any(_m42e_detect_primary_for_anchor(row, a) for a in anchors)
 1097: 
 1098:     def _authority(row: dict[str, Any]) -> float:
 1099:         # Default 1.0 ONLY when authority_score is absent/None — an EXPLICIT 0.0
 1100:         # (genuinely zero-authority row) must rank as 0.0, not be laundered to 1.0
 1101:         # by a falsy `or` (Codex diff-gate P2).
 1102:         a = row.get("authority_score")
 1103:         return 1.0 if a is None else float(a)
 1104: 
 1105:     kept = [
 1106:         item for item in scored
 1107:         if item[1] >= relevance_floor or _is_anchor(item[3])
 1108:     ]
 1109:     kept.sort(
 1110:         key=lambda s: (
 1111:             -(s[1] * _authority(s[3])),
 1112:             _TIER_PRIORITY.get(s[2], 9),
 1113:             s[0],
 1114:         )
 1115:     )
 1116:     selected_rows: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
 1117:     selected_counts: dict[str, int] = {}
 1118:     for idx, score, tier, row in kept:
 1119:         new_row = dict(row)
 1120:         new_row["selection_relevance"] = float(score)
 1121:         selected_rows.append(new_row)
 1122:         selected_counts[tier] = selected_counts.get(tier, 0) + 1
 1123:     anchor_exempt = sum(
 1124:         1 for item in kept
 1125:         if item[1] < relevance_floor and _is_anchor(item[3])
 1126:     )
 1127:     return EvidenceSelection(
 1128:         selected_rows=selected_rows,
 1129:         full_counts=full_counts,
 1130:         selected_counts=selected_counts,
 1131:         dropped_count=len(scored) - len(kept),
 1132:         selection_strategy="relevance_floor_v1",
 1133:         notes=[
 1134:             f"relevance_floor={relevance_floor}: kept {len(kept)}/{len(scored)} "
 1135:             f"rows (>= floor OR primary anchor); no max_rows cap; ranked "
 1136:             f"relevance x authority_score; anchor_floor_exempt={anchor_exempt}",
 1137:         ],
 1138:     )

 1141: def select_evidence_for_generation(
 1142:     *,
 1143:     research_question: str,
 1144:     protocol: dict[str, Any] | None,
 1145:     classified_sources: list[Any],
 1146:     evidence_rows: list[dict[str, Any]],
 1147:     max_rows: int,
 1148:     primary_trial_anchors: list[str] | None = None,
 1149:     relevance_floor: float | None = None,
 1150:     sub_queries: list[str] | None = None,
 1151: ) -> EvidenceSelection:
 1152:     """Pick up to max_rows evidence rows, tier-balanced + relevance-ranked.
 1153: 
 1154:     Strategy (deterministic, stable):
 1155:       1. Join evidence rows to classified sources by URL → tier per row.
 1156:       2. Compute full_tier_counts.
 1157:       3. Score each row by lexical relevance (question+protocol anchors
 1158:          vs statement+direct_quote content words).
 1159:       4. Sort within each tier by (-relevance, original_index) for
 1160:          deterministic top-N per tier.
 1161:       5. Allocate slots proportionally to full_tier_counts but with
 1162:          floors for high-value tiers that are present (each T1-T3 tier
 1163:          present gets at least min(1, available)).
 1164:       6. Fill remaining slots globally by (tier_priority, -relevance,
 1165:          original_index).
 1166: 
 1167:     Args:
 1168:         research_question: the user's raw question.
 1169:         protocol: scope protocol dict (uses population/intervention/
 1170:             comparator/outcome/research_question if present).
 1171:         classified_sources: list of objects with .url and .tier (or
 1172:             dict-like with those keys). Used for URL→tier join.
 1173:         evidence_rows: full evidence row pool.
 1174:         max_rows: target selection size.
 1175: 
 1176:     Returns:
 1177:         EvidenceSelection with selected_rows + telemetry.
 1178:     """
 1179:     # I-meta-005 Phase 5 (#989) — Codex diff-gate P2: in relevance-floor mode the
 1180:     # `max_rows` cap is REPLACED by the floor, so a legacy `max_rows <= 0` must NOT
 1181:     # empty the ON-mode pool. The empty-corpus guard still applies in both modes.
 1182:     if (max_rows <= 0 and relevance_floor is None) or not evidence_rows:
 1183:         return EvidenceSelection(
 1184:             selected_rows=[], full_counts={}, selected_counts={},
 1185:             dropped_count=0, selection_strategy="tier_balanced_v1",
 1186:             notes=["no evidence rows or max_rows <= 0"],
 1187:         )
 1188: 
 1189:     # Build URL → tier map from classified_sources (whatever shape).
 1190:     url_to_tier: dict[str, str] = {}
 1191:     for src in classified_sources or []:
 1192:         url = getattr(src, "url", None) or (
 1193:             src.get("url") if isinstance(src, dict) else None
 1194:         )
 1195:         tier = getattr(src, "tier", None) or (
 1196:             src.get("tier") if isinstance(src, dict) else None
 1197:         )
 1198:         if url and tier:
 1199:             url_to_tier[str(url)] = str(tier)
 1200: 
 1201:     # Compute relevance tokens from research question + protocol anchors.
 1202:     question_tokens = _content_tokens(research_question or "")
 1203:     protocol_tokens: set[str] = set()
 1204:     if protocol:
 1205:         for key in ("population", "intervention", "comparator",

  735: def _row_query_origin(row: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
  736:     """The sub-query that surfaced the row (`query_origin`), or `_unlabeled`."""
  737:     return str(row.get("query_origin") or "") or "_unlabeled"

  739: 
  740: def _row_domain(row: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
  741:     """Registrable-ish domain (last two host labels) for the per-domain cap."""
  742:     url = (row.get("source_url") or row.get("url") or "").lower()
  743:     if not url:
  744:         return ""
  745:     try:
  746:         from urllib.parse import urlparse
  747:         host = (urlparse(url if "://" in url else f"http://{url}").hostname or "").lower()
  748:     except (ValueError, AttributeError):
  749:         return ""
  750:     parts = host.split(".")
  751:     return ".".join(parts[-2:]) if len(parts) >= 2 else host

  754: def _priority_sort_key(
  755:     item: tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]],
  756:     rec_enabled: bool,
  757:     rec_eps: float,
  758: ) -> tuple:
  759:     """Within-tier priority for diversity swap decisions — same ordering the
  760:     selector uses (banded relevance + #955 recency, then original index).
  761:     Ascending = best first; max(...) of this key = worst (lowest priority)."""
  762:     return (*_relevance_recency_key(item, rec_enabled, rec_eps), item[0])

  765: def _reserve_subqueries(
  766:     selected: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
  767:     scored: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
  768:     protected_ids: set[int],
  769:     k: int,
  770:     rec_enabled: bool,
  771:     rec_eps: float,
  772: ) -> tuple[int, set[int]]:
  773:     """Same-tier swap so each sub-query origin present in a tier's pool reaches
  774:     >= k selected rows in that tier, evicting only NON-protected slack rows whose
  775:     origin is OVER-represented (count > k). Returns (swaps, brought_in_ids)."""
  776:     selected_ids = {id(it) for it in selected}
  777:     pool_by_tier: dict[str, list] = defaultdict(list)
  778:     for it in scored:
  779:         if id(it) not in selected_ids:
  780:             pool_by_tier[it[2]].append(it)
  781:     brought_in: set[int] = set()
  782:     swaps = 0
  783:     for tier in {it[2] for it in selected}:
  784:         sel_pos = [i for i, it in enumerate(selected) if it[2] == tier]
  785:         origin_count = Counter(_row_query_origin(selected[i][3]) for i in sel_pos)
  786:         cand_by_origin: dict[str, list] = defaultdict(list)
  787:         for it in pool_by_tier.get(tier, []):
  788:             cand_by_origin[_row_query_origin(it[3])].append(it)
  789:         missing = sorted(
  790:             [o for o in cand_by_origin if origin_count.get(o, 0) < k],
  791:             key=lambda o: (-max(c[1] for c in cand_by_origin[o]), o),
  792:         )
  793:         for origin in missing:
  794:             cands = sorted(
  795:                 cand_by_origin[origin],
  796:                 key=lambda c: _priority_sort_key(c, rec_enabled, rec_eps),
  797:             )
  798:             ci = 0
  799:             while origin_count.get(origin, 0) < k and ci < len(cands):
  800:                 evictable = [
  801:                     i for i in sel_pos
  802:                     if id(selected[i]) not in protected_ids
  803:                     and id(selected[i]) not in brought_in
  804:                     and origin_count[_row_query_origin(selected[i][3])] > k
  805:                     and _row_query_origin(selected[i][3]) != origin
  806:                 ]
  807:                 if not evictable:
  808:                     break
  809:                 evict_i = max(
  810:                     evictable,
  811:                     key=lambda i: _priority_sort_key(selected[i], rec_enabled, rec_eps),
  812:                 )
  813:                 bring = cands[ci]
  814:                 ci += 1
  815:                 old_origin = _row_query_origin(selected[evict_i][3])
  816:                 selected[evict_i] = bring
  817:                 brought_in.add(id(bring))
  818:                 origin_count[old_origin] -= 1
  819:                 origin_count[origin] = origin_count.get(origin, 0) + 1
  820:                 swaps += 1
  821:     return swaps, brought_in
  822: 
  823: 
  824: def _apply_domain_cap(
  825:     selected: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
  826:     scored: list[tuple[int, float, str, dict[str, Any]]],
  827:     protected_ids: set[int],
  828:     cap: int,
  829:     rec_enabled: bool,
  830:     rec_eps: float,
  831: ) -> int:
  832:     """Soft per-domain cap via same-tier swaps. An over-cap domain's NON-protected
  833:     slack rows (worst-priority first) are replaced by the best same-tier pool row
  834:     of an under-cap domain. YIELDS (stops) when no valid same-tier replacement
  835:     exists — never leaves a slot empty, drops a tier below quota, or evicts a
  836:     protected/reserved row. Returns the number of rows moved."""
  837:     moved = 0
  838:     selected_ids = {id(it) for it in selected}
  839:     domain_count = Counter(_row_domain(it[3]) for it in selected)
  840:     over = [d for d, c in domain_count.items() if d and c > cap]
  841:     for dom in over:
  842:         while domain_count[dom] > cap:
  843:             evict_positions = sorted(
  844:                 [i for i, it in enumerate(selected)
  845:                  if _row_domain(it[3]) == dom and id(it) not in protected_ids],
  846:                 key=lambda i: _priority_sort_key(selected[i], rec_enabled, rec_eps),
  847:                 reverse=True,  # worst-priority first
  848:             )
  849:             swapped = False
  850:             for ei in evict_positions:
  851:                 tier = selected[ei][2]
  852:                 repls = [
  853:                     it for it in scored
  854:                     if id(it) not in selected_ids
  855:                     and it[2] == tier
  856:                     and _row_domain(it[3]) != dom
  857:                     and domain_count.get(_row_domain(it[3]), 0) < cap
  858:                 ]
  859:                 if not repls:
  860:                     continue
  861:                 best = min(repls, key=lambda it: _priority_sort_key(it, rec_enabled, rec_eps))
  862:                 old = selected[ei]
  863:                 selected[ei] = best
  864:                 selected_ids.discard(id(old))
  865:                 selected_ids.add(id(best))
  866:                 domain_count[dom] -= 1
  867:                 domain_count[_row_domain(best[3])] = domain_count.get(_row_domain(best[3]), 0) + 1
  868:                 moved += 1
  869:                 swapped = True
  870:                 break
  871:             if not swapped:
  872:                 break  # yield: no same-tier under-cap replacement available
  873:     return moved
  874:

  876: def _m46_short_pool_ordered_selection(

  882:     primary_trial_anchors: list[str] | None,
  883: ) -> EvidenceSelection:
  884:     """M-46 (2026-04-22): compute floor detection + deterministic
  885:     priority ordering + telemetry for the short-pool case
  886:     (pool_size <= max_rows).

  888:     Pre-M-46: the early-exit branch returned evidence_rows as-is with

  896:     audits see consistent signals regardless of pool size.
  897: 
  898:     The function is self-contained and does NOT share state with the
  899:     main branch; it re-detects primaries / mechanism / HC using the

  904:         f"pool_size<=max_rows ({len(scored)}/{max_rows})",
  905:         "m46_short_pool_ordered_selection",
  906:     ]
  907: 
  908:     # --- M-42e primary detection ---
  909:     m42e_ids: set[int] = set()

  912:         # Prefer T1 primaries; fall back to any-tier if no T1 match
  913:         # (matches M-42e spirit — the primary paper is the priority).
  914:         t1_scored = [s for s in scored if s[2] == "T1"]
  915:         for anchor in primary_trial_anchors:
  916:             if len(m42e_ids) >= _M42E_PRIMARY_FLOOR_CAP:
  917:                 break
  918:             matched = False
  919:             for item in t1_scored:
  920:                 if id(item) in m42e_ids:
  921:                     continue
  922:                 if _m42e_detect_primary_for_anchor(item[3], anchor):
  923:                     m42e_ids.add(id(item))
  924:                     m42e_anchors.append(anchor)
  925:                     matched = True
  926:                     break
  927:             # No fallback to other tiers — same contract as main branch.
  928:             del matched  # quiet linters
  929:     if m42e_anchors:
  930:         notes.append(

 2951: 
 2952: def _m42b_year_from_row(row: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
 2953:     """Extract publication year from an evidence row. Tries URL/DOI
 2954:     year pattern, then direct_quote, then refetched quote (M-42b
 2955:     pass-2 medium). Returns 'yyyy' or empty string."""
 2956:     url = (row.get("source_url") or row.get("url") or "")
 2957:     # Common DOI/URL year patterns: /2021/, (2021), -2021-
 2958:     m = re.search(r"[/(\-_](20\d{2})[/)\-_.]", url)
 2959:     if m:
 2960:         return m.group(1)
 2961:     quote = row.get("direct_quote") or ""
 2962:     m = re.search(r"\b(20[0-2]\d)\b", quote[:500])
 2963:     if m:
 2964:         return m.group(1)
 2965:     # Pass-2 medium: check refetched quote when original was thin.
 2966:     refetched = row.get("_m42b_refetched_quote") or ""
 2967:     m = re.search(r"\b(20[0-2]\d)\b", refetched[:500])
 2968:     if m:
 2969:         return m.group(1)
 2970:     return ""
 2971: 
 2972: 
 2973: def _m42b_find_ref_num(row: dict[str, Any], bibliography: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> int | None:
 2974:     """Return the [N] citation number from the bibliography that
 2975:     corresponds to this evidence row. Match by evidence_id or URL."""
 2976:     ev_id = row.get("evidence_id") or ""
 2977:     url = row.get("source_url") or row.get("url") or ""
 2978:     for entry in bibliography:
 2979:         if entry.get("evidence_id") == ev_id and ev_id:
 2980:             return entry.get("num")
 2981:         if entry.get("url") == url and url:
 2982:             return entry.get("num")
 2983:     return None
 2984: 
 2985: 
 2986: def build_trial_summary_and_timeline_from_evidence(
 2987:     selected_rows: list[dict[str, Any]],
 2988:     primary_trial_anchors: list[str],
 2989:     bibliography: list[dict[str, Any]],
 2990:     refetch_fn: Any = None,
 2991:     *,
 2992:     refetch_diagnostics_sink: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None,
 2993: ) -> tuple[str, str]:
 2994:     """M-42b deterministic builder. Consumes selected evidence rows
 2995:     (from the generator's evidence_pool) + the sweep's
 2996:     `primary_trial_anchors` list + global bibliography. Returns
 2997:     `(trial_table_md, timeline_md)` — both markdown strings.
 2998: 
 2999:     Source-content contract (per Codex plan review pass-3):
 3000:       - Primary extraction source: `row.get("direct_quote")` — the
 3001:         verbatim quote populated by live_retriever during fetch.
 3002:       - Secondary: `row.get("statement")` for disambiguation only.
 3003:       - Forbidden: prose from any generated report section.
 3004: 
 3005:     Thin-content fallback:
 3006:       - If `direct_quote` < 100 chars AND `refetch_fn` is provided,
 3007:         calls `refetch_fn(url)` to fetch a fresh 2000-char extract.
 3008:       - If still thin, the row is marked extraction-ineligible
 3009:         (skipped).
 3010: 
 3011:     Row acceptance:
 3012:       - For the TABLE: >=4 of 7 frame cells populated.
 3013:       - For the TIMELINE: publication year + trial name + at least
 3014:         one non-empty cell (endpoint OR effect).
 3015: 
 3016:     Returns empty strings when no rows pass the threshold (caller
 3017:     falls back to LLM path).
 3018:     """
